interpretations.interpretation: The Maoist sacred political order established 'the people' as an eternal collective body whose continuation gave meaning to individual death. Mao's explicit contrast — death for the people is weightier than Mount Tai, death for exploiters is lighter than a feather — encodes a graded hierarchy of sacrifice in which only death that serves collective immortality is meaningful. Selflessness is not merely praised but structurally required.

confidence.label: moderate-high
confidence.rationale: Both passages are core canonical texts of the Maoist sacred political order. Chain is thin but directly grounded in verified primary sources. Pending periodization review and chain expansion.

interpretations.mechanismSummary: The Maoist sacred political order represented 'the People' as a transcendent collective body; Mao's canonical texts explicitly coded death-for-the-people as the supreme form of human meaning, structurally demanding individual self-abnegation.

claims.claim: Maoist political language structured individual life and death as meaningful only through service to 'the people' as an eternal collective body, inverting private survival as the supreme value and demanding selfless self-abnegation — including willingness to die — as the highest moral obligation.

confidence.label: moderate-high
confidence.rationale: Both passages are from Mao's Selected Works (public domain, verified via Marxists.org). 'Serve the People' explicitly codes death-for-the-people as weightier than personal survival. 'In Memory of Bethune' codes absolute selflessness as the highest virtue. Together they establish the collective-immortality-to-sacrifice mechanism at the level of the sacred political order's core texts. Confidence capped pending broader periodization review.

passages.locator: Mao Zedong, 'Serve the People,' 8 September 1944; in Selected Works of Mao Tse-tung, Vol. 3 (Peking: Foreign Languages Press, 1965), p. 227; text verified at https://www.marxists.org/reference/archive/mao/selected-works/volume-3/mswv3_19.htm
passages.text: To die for the people is weightier than Mount Tai, but to work for the fascists and die for the exploiters and oppressors is lighter than a feather.

source.notes: Public domain. Selected Works Vol. 3, verified at marxists.org.

passages.locator: Mao Zedong, 'In Memory of Norman Bethune,' 21 December 1939; in Selected Works of Mao Tse-tung, Vol. 2 (Peking: Foreign Languages Press, 1965), p. 337; text verified at https://www.marxists.org/reference/archive/mao/selected-works/volume-2/mswv2_25.htm
passages.text: We must all learn the spirit of absolute selflessness from him. With this spirit everyone can be very useful to the people.

source.notes: Public domain. Selected Works Vol. 2, verified at marxists.org.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Maximum collective-immortality score is directly supported by Mao's canonical texts coding death-for-the-people as the supreme human meaning. Confidence capped at moderate pending chain expansion and periodization review.

scores.reviewNote: Score value 5/5 defensible: collective-immortality mechanism (revolutionary martyrdom, continuous revolution doctrine) supports maximum score. Confidence 0.67 reflects chain-requires-expansion and periodization ambiguity (which phase of Maoism?). Hold at score-review until chain expansion and periodization is specified.
